chords.name: Visceral Hit
chords.formula_human: Gut reaction is high (top 30% of clip) AND cognitive effort is low (bottom 30%) — held for at least 1 second.
chords.meaning: The body reacts before the mind catches up. The anterior insula — the cortex's interoceptive hub, mapping body-state signals into felt sensation — fires high while the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex stays quiet. This pattern is the cortical signature of a moment that arrives in the chest before the brain has decided whether to care. Pre-reflective. Bypasses analysis.

chords.name: Learning Moment
chords.formula_human: Attention and memory encoding are both high (top 30% of clip) — held for at least 1 second.
chords.meaning: The cortex is encoding what will be remembered. The dorsal attention network and the left ventrolateral prefrontal cortex co-activate — sustained attention plus the subsequent-memory effect (Wagner 1998) firing together. When these two systems peak together, the brain is doing more than processing: it is committing the moment to long-term storage. The signature of content built to stick.

chords.name: Reasoning Beat
chords.formula_human: Cognitive effort and language depth are both high (top 30% of clip) — held for at least 2 seconds.
chords.meaning: The viewer is actively interpreting. The dorsolateral prefrontal cortex applies cognitive control while the language network — Broca's and Wernicke's together — processes semantic meaning under that control. This isn't passive listening; the cortex is restructuring its mental model in real time. The signature of content that asks the viewer to think rather than feel.

chords.name: Emotional Impact
chords.formula_human: Self-relevance and gut reaction are both high (top 30% of clip) — held for at least 1 second.
chords.meaning: The medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) and the anterior insula co-activate — self-relevance and visceral response firing simultaneously. The cortex is saying "this matters to me, in my body." This is the exact pattern Falk and colleagues found in 2012 predicted population-level behavior change at r=0.87 in their anti-smoking ad study. The signature of content that doesn't just inform but lands.

chords.name: Social Resonance
chords.formula_human: Theory-of-mind and self-relevance are both high (top 30% of clip) — held for at least 1 second.
chords.meaning: The right temporo-parietal junction and the medial prefrontal cortex co-activate — the cortex is modeling another mind while asking how it relates to the self. This is the cortical pattern under parasocial bonding: the moment a viewer stops watching a creator and starts feeling like they know them. The signature of content that builds relationship, not just attention.

chords.name: Cold Cognitive Work
chords.formula_human: Cognitive effort is high (top 30%) AND self-relevance is low (bottom 30%) — held for at least 2 seconds.
chords.meaning: The dorsolateral prefrontal cortex works hard while the medial prefrontal cortex stays quiet. The cortex is processing without integrating — solving a problem the viewer doesn't yet feel is theirs. The signature of content that informs but does not yet land.

chords.name: Story Integration
chords.formula: Memory >= theta_S AND Social >= theta_S AND Language >= theta_S
chords.formula_human: Memory, theory-of-mind, and language are all at or above the median — held for at least 2 seconds.
chords.meaning: Three systems coordinate: memory encoding what's happening, theory-of-mind tracking who's doing it, and the language network processing how it's being told. This is the cortical signature of the brain treating content as a story rather than a series of moments — the network state under sustained narrative comprehension.

citations_registry.craig-2009: Craig, A. D. (2009). How do you feel — now? The anterior insula and human awareness. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 10(1), 59–70.
citations_registry.critchley-2005: Critchley, H. D. (2005). Neural mechanisms of autonomic, affective, and cognitive integration. Journal of Comparative Neurology, 493(1), 154–166.
citations_registry.wagner-1998: Wagner, A. D., et al. (1998). Building memories: remembering and forgetting of verbal experiences as predicted by brain activity. Science, 281, 1188–1191.
citations_registry.corbetta-shulman-2002: Corbetta, M., & Shulman, G. L. (2002). Control of goal-directed and stimulus-driven attention in the brain. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 3, 201–215.
citations_registry.uncapher-wagner-2009: Uncapher, M. R., & Wagner, A. D. (2009). Posterior parietal cortex and episodic encoding. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 10, 613–625.
citations_registry.miller-cohen-2001: Miller, E. K., & Cohen, J. D. (2001). An integrative theory of prefrontal cortex function. Annual Review of Neuroscience, 24, 167–202.
citations_registry.fedorenko-2011: Fedorenko, E., Behr, M. K., & Kanwisher, N. (2011). Functional specificity for high-level linguistic processing in the human brain. PNAS, 108(39), 16428–16433.
citations_registry.falk-2012: Falk, E. B., Berkman, E. T., & Lieberman, M. D. (2012). From neural responses to population behavior. Psychological Science, 23(5), 439–445.
citations_registry.northoff-2006: Northoff, G., et al. (2006). Self-referential processing in our brain — a meta-analysis. NeuroImage, 31(1), 440–457.
citations_registry.saxe-kanwisher-2003: Saxe, R., & Kanwisher, N. (2003). People thinking about thinking people. NeuroImage, 19(4), 1835–1842.
citations_registry.mar-2011: Mar, R. A. (2011). The neural bases of social cognition and story comprehension. Annual Review of Psychology, 62, 103–134.
